coredata.dc:title: Size and chemistry of 1–2 layer graphene nanosheets at the transition into photoluminescence and assembly with large shifts from blue to red emission

coredata.dc:description: Synthetic refinement of graphene photoluminescence remains challenging because underlying characteristics have not been well understood from polydisperse mixtures of different sizes and surface chemistries. Here, we isolate and characterise different populations of 1–2 layer graphene (1-2LG) each with narrow size range. Small 1-2LG nanosheets down to 25 nm size remained black and non-luminescent with N. and O. additions at their edges, whereas 15 nm nanosheets transitioned into photoluminescence, while retaining substantial sp2 carbon of graphene. Positively-charged amine modifications overcame the repulsion between nanosheets from the negative zeta potential of sp2 graphene faces in water and allowed assembly of similarly-sized nanosheets with large red shifts (>150 nm) taking blue emission into the red. However, more extensive edge modification into carboxyl groups and neutral amides built negative charge at the edges. Together with smaller nanosheet size and reduced sp2 carbon, red shifts were weakened by half in these isolates, which were most modified and more like graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots. N addition to form amines at nanosheet edges, while avoiding O atom addition and carboxyl groups, presents as a simple refinement for synthesis of self-assembling graphene quantum dots with multi-colour emission, which also retain sp2 carbon properties of graphene.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of ultrasonic exfoliation and isolation of different nanosheets: upper cartoon of possible steps of exfoliation and fragmentation driven by cavitation forces and associated sonochemistry. Lower schematic of fractionation stages: centrifugation (from right to left) removing large unexfoliated graphite (GT) at 3,000 g, harvesting larger graphene nanosheets (LG) at 17,000 g and small graphene (SG) nanosheets at 58,000 g. The remaining dispersion of smaller nanosheets and dots were fractionated into nanosheet assemblies retained (R) and nanosheets and dots dialysing (D), when each was separated using high performance Superdex 75 size-exclusion chromatography (SEC) into 3 distinct fractions.

TEM images of isolated GNM fractions: (a) larger FLG (LG) nanosheets harvested by centrifugation, leaving supernatant fractions of (b) smaller 1-2LG nanosheets RF1, (c) yellow fractions RF2 and (d) RF3 retained by dialysis, and dialysate fractions (e) DF1, (f) DF2, and (g) DF3, separated by size-exclusion FPLC. Upper inset is the HR-TEM image (bar 2 nm), which shows the lattice spacing, and the lower inset is the size distribution. (h) Summary of GNMs lateral sizes including standard deviations (95 %).

AFM height scans of isolated GNM fractions: (a) larger FLG (LG) nanosheets, fractions retained by dialysis (b) smaller 1-2LG nanosheets RF1, (c) RF2 and (d) RF3, and dialysed fractions (e) DF1, (f) DF2, and (g) DF3, separated by size-exclusion FPLC. (h) Ranked thickness (number of layers) of fractions.

1H NMR (left) and 13C NMR (right) spectra of 1-2LG nanosheets isolated in D2O: from top to bottom, fractions retained by dialysis (RF2, red & RF3, blue) and dialysate fractions (DF1, dark green; DF2, purple; DF3, orange).

XPS, FTIR, Raman and Zeta Potential of isolated nanosheets and dots: (a) XPS spectra of sonochemical modifications of graphitic C (1s peak at 285.0) with oxygen (1s peak at 532 eV) and nitrogen (1s peak at 400 eV), showing atomic contributions (%) of C, O and N, (b) FTIR spectra and (c) Raman spectra of isolated GNM fractions, from top to bottom, larger FLG (LG) nanosheets (black), fractions retained by dialysis smaller 1-2LG nanosheets RF1 (bright green), RF2 (red) and RF3 (blue), and dialysate fractions (DF1, dark green; DF2, purple; DF3, orange). The D, G and 2D peaks were fitted with a Gaussian function (inset ratio of D and G intensities). (d) Zeta potential of isolated GNM fractions at pH 7: ζ values were calculated as averages ± SD of three measurements.

Optical properties (a) UV–Vis absorption of GNMs and larger FLG (LG) nanosheet (right inset) and blue photoluminescence under 360 nm lamp (left inset, from left to right, RF2, RF3, DF1, DF2, DF3) and (b to f) photoluminescence (PL) spectra upon excitation ranging from 300 nm to 500 nm for nanosheets retained by dialysis RF2 (b) & RF3 (c) and nanosheets in the dialysate DF1 (d), DF2 (e) & DF3 (f). (g) PL spectra of the nanosheet fractions excited at 340 nm. The right upper inset is a normalised PL spectra expanding the spectral shifts. (h) Excitation spectra of the nanosheet fractions for photoluminescence emission measured at 440 nm. The left upper inset is a schematic energy level diagram of possible mechanism of nanosheet photoluminescence.

Concentration-dependent emission spectra. Red shift of emission spectra after excitation at 340 nm with increasing concentrations (traces left to right 0.025 (blue), 0.05 (green), 0.5 (orange), 1 mg/ml (red)) of (a) RF2, (b) RF3, (c) DF1, (d) DF2 and (e) DF3 with the corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates of emission spectra (f-j, respectively).

head.ce:title: Size and chemistry of 1–2 layer graphene nanosheets at the transition into photoluminescence and assembly with large shifts from blue to red emission

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthetic refinement of graphene photoluminescence remains challenging because underlying characteristics have not been well understood from polydisperse mixtures of different sizes and surface chemistries. Here, we isolate and characterise different populations of 1–2 layer graphene (1-2LG) each with narrow size range. Small 1-2LG nanosheets down to 25 nm size remained black and non-luminescent with N. and O. additions at their edges, whereas 15 nm nanosheets transitioned into photoluminescence, while retaining substantial sp2 carbon of graphene. Positively-charged amine modifications overcame the repulsion between nanosheets from the negative zeta potential of sp2 graphene faces in water and allowed assembly of similarly-sized nanosheets with large red shifts (>150 nm) taking blue emission into the red. However, more extensive edge modification into carboxyl groups and neutral amides built negative charge at the edges. Together with smaller nanosheet size and reduced sp2 carbon, red shifts were weakened by half in these isolates, which were most modified and more like graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots. N addition to form amines at nanosheet edges, while avoiding O atom addition and carboxyl groups, presents as a simple refinement for synthesis of self-assembling graphene quantum dots with multi-colour emission, which also retain sp2 carbon properties of graphene.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Interest in graphene nanomaterials (GNMs) is driven by their outstanding morphological, mechanical, and electrical properties [1,2]. Extensive theoretical and experimental studies have explored graphene and its derivatives. Although different applications benefit (at least theoretically) from graphene with particular properties (size, thickness, structure and edge chemistries), polydisperse mixtures are often used experimentally. Remarkable quantum confinement and edge effects arise when two-dimensional pristine graphene nanosheets with a zero-band gap become zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots with covalent modifications resulting in non-zero-band gap [3,4]. Interest in graphene quantum dots has exploded in electrochemical sensors [5], light-emitting diodes [6], solar cells [7], batteries [8], photocatalysis [9], fluorophores and bioimaging [10], drug delivery [11].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are often not carefully distinguished [3,4]. CQDs are small (<3 nm) spheres of amorphous sp2 carbon, some with a crystalline core [12]. Rather than from graphene, GQDs are produced top-down from strongly-oxidized graphene oxide with base plane defects and substantial sp3 bonding, sacrificing the electronic properties of graphene [3]. 2D graphene nanosheets synthesized (bottom up) or cleaved (top down) to quantum confinement size scales are rarely considered, other than for electronic devices, as they are seen as not easily synthesised, dispersed in solvents and functionalised [3,4]. However, retaining the electrical conductivity, charge transfer, π-π bonding and adsorption properties of graphene would be useful in many other applications too. Hence, we isolated and characterised here different 1–2 layer graphene (1-2LG) nanosheets that were progressively cut down and edge modified to the transition from 25 nm to 15 nm into quantum-confined edge effects of 2D nanosheets, into smaller nanosheets and zero-dimensional (0D) dots (1 – 2 nm).

Cutting of precursors down to the nanoscale by solvothermal or hydrothermal, acidic or plasma oxidation and liquid-phase exfoliation produces graphene nanomaterials at useful quality, scales and cost, including heterogenous dispersions of GQDs [13–15]. Ultrasonication has enjoyed most use for the exfoliation graphite flakes into colloidal dispersions of few-layer graphene [16,17]. The collapse of cavitation bubbles in fluctuating pressure waves produces shock waves and high-speed microjets that overcome van der Waals forces to exfoliate graphite layers [17–19] and then fragment exfoliating layers into flakes [20], with only bulk controls of sonication time, power, solvent, dispersion volume and temperature [21]. However, cavitation also produces gas radicals performing N and O atom addition at the edges, followed by progressive further modification, which allowed isolation of different stages of transition into quantum-confined edge effects.

N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) remains the most effective solvent to exfoliate graphene [22], notably because its energy surface is similar to that of graphene [23]. NMP also has a relatively low cavitation threshold [22] at the ultrasonic frequencies (<100 kHz) typically used. Given its relatively high boiling point, dissolved gases likely allow dominance of gas cavitation in NMP, over weaker vaporous cavitation possible in more volatile and degassed solvents. Cavitation increases in NMP when graphite flakes are added, presumably by adsorbing gas and nucleating gas cavitation bubbles. However, cavitation bubbles are observed landing and imploding on graphene surfaces, rather than nucleating there, even when presented on graphite [22]. Indeed, rather than hydrophobic gas adsorption and bubble nucleation sites, non-functionalised graphene surfaces are mildly hydrophilic and negatively charged in polar solvents, like NMP and water [24]. However, smaller nanosheet edges can be preferentially attacked by short-lived gas radicals, indicating that gas bubbles are nucleated and imploded in their proximity. NMP, like other solvents and solutes undergoes sonochemical degradation and polymerisation, which may modify graphene [25], but here far less than by direct attack by gas radicals from localised bubble implosion.

Defined by quantum confinement (<10–15 nm size for near single layer), different edge chemistries affect the electronic transitions and band gap, and the resulting electronic and optical properties of GQDs [26–28]. Microscopy (AFM, TEM) has extensively analysed the structural properties of individual flakes and nanosheets within polydisperse preparations, as their separation is difficult [16], but heterogeneous mixtures are chemically analysed and otherwise used. Indeed, recent photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy demonstrated that, while individual GQDs had a narrow emission, the emission bands of different GQDs varied, resulting in the broad emission [29]. The need for improved purification and new chemistry avenues for synthesis of GQDs were emphasised then as a strategy for production of homogeneous quantum dots [29]. Here, alongside the well-studied structural properties informing mechanical processes [17–19], we used high speed centrifugation to isolate few-layer flakes (75 nm) and small nanosheets (25 nm) of 1-2LG, and high performance chromatography to separate smaller 1-2LG nanosheets and their assemblies, based on different sizes and extents of edge modifications generated upon persistent gas radical attack. Additional to XPS and IR generally relied upon, solution-phase NMR spectroscopy resolved key edge functionalisation differences. Although similarly edge-modified and within the quantum confinement range, ∼ 75 nm and ∼ 25 nm 1-2LG nanosheets were not photoluminescent, whereas nanosheets cut to ∼ 15 nm and below showed similar excitation-dependent emissions, irrespective of the differences in the range of O and N functional groups. However, the red shifts from concentration-dependent assembly of nanosheets were much stronger for early N⋅ radical attack with positively-charged edge modifications retaining substantial sp2 carbon, than for later highly-oxidised, fragmented or dot-like nanosheets comparable to GQDs and CQDs.

Natural graphite flakes were of Grade 2369 from Branwell Graphite Ltd., UK. Cellulose dialysis tubing (diameter 21 mm, SV-44114-01, Enzo Life Sciences Ltd., UK) retained small nanoparticles (MWCO 12,400 Da). Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, E9884), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, 99 %), deuterium oxide (D2O; 99.9 % grade, 151882) and sulfuric acid (99 %, 339741) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (UK), and quinine sulphate (QS, AS-80040) from Generon Ltd., UK.

Graphite flakes were sieved (500 mesh) to remove large graphite particles, which may not be exfoliated completely by low power sonication. Sieved flakes had an average lateral dimension of approx. 200 µm and Raman ID / IG ∼ 0.08 of graphite. Portions (125 mg) were exfoliated by low power sonication (32 W, 37 kHz, 30 % power) for up to 60 h directly into NMP (25 ml) in round-bottom flasks (100 ml) placed 2 to 3 cm above the centre of a transducer of a bath sonicator (Elmasonic P, Elma Schmidbauer GmbH, Germany). A refrigerated cooling coil maintained the temperature of the sonicator bath between 19 °C and 23 °C. The grey-black dispersions were centrifuged first at 3,200 g (4000 rpm, Centra-CL3R Refrigerated Centrifuge) for 20 min at room temperature to remove unexfoliated graphite materials as pellets. Supernatants were carefully collected with a glass Pasteur pipette and centrifuged further for 20 min at 17,418 g, (12,000 rpm, JA-30.50 rotor, Beckman Coulter Avanti J-25 I high-speed centrifuge) at room temperature, when the pellets were retained as larger few-layer graphene (LG) nanosheets. The resulting supernatant was further clarified at 58,545 g (22,000 rpm), before evaporation of NMP at 40 °C for 24 h (Genevac EZ-2 EElite Evaporator) to yield a yellow waxy residue containing 1–2 layer graphene (1-2LG) nanosheets, which were immediately dispersed in deionised (DI) water as a yellow solution, stable for at least 6 months without sedimentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent exchange and fractionation by dialysis
ce:section.ce:para: As NMP is miscible in water, dialysis provided effective solvent exchange for 1-2LG dispersions, particularly to replace NMP with water, and to separate small nanosheets and dots. Dialysis tubing was prepared by boiling in aqueous EDTA (1 mM) for 10 min, followed by rinsing in deionised (DI) water, when it was further boiled in DI water for 10 min and then washed with copious volumes of DI water before use.

Grey-black LG dispersions in NMP were introduced into dialysis tubing, sealed tightly with a knot, and dialysed against 3 successive 10-fold volumes of DI water, each for 12 h at room temperature, assisted by gentle magnetic stirring. For characterisation of these preparations by NMR spectroscopy, DI water dispersions were further dialysed against six successive three-fold volumes of D2O. Aqueous FLG dispersions were physically similar to the starting NMP dispersions of FLG.

Yellow 1-2LG nanosheet and dot solutions were extensively dialysed against 3 successive 10-fold volumes of deionised water, each for 48 h at room temperature and assisted by gentle magnetic stirring. The fraction retained inside the dialysis tubing and fraction dialysing through to the outside of the dialysis tubing solution from all 3 dialysis cycles were collected and frozen separately in liquid nitrogen before lyophilisation (E-C Micromodulo freeze dryer, with a Savant VLP 120 pump).

The lyophilised preparations were each reconstituted in DI water before passage through a high performance size-exclusion chromatography column (HiLoad Superdex 75 prep grade packed with 34 μm matrix of dextran and highly cross-linked agarose) using a Fast Protein Liquid chromatography (FPLC) system (ÄKTA Avant controlled by UNICORN software, General Electric Healthcare) operating at a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min of 20 % (v/v) methanol/DI with UV detection at 280 nm. Collected fractions were lyophilised for subsequent characterisation. For NMR analysis, the required amounts of the lyophilised 1-2LG nanosheet samples were dispersed in 600 μl D2O.

Concentrations of LG dispersions were estimated from UV–Vis spectra using the Lambert-Beer law relating measured absorbance to concentration (mg ml−1) with absorption coefficient of 4632 ml mg−1 m−1 at 660 nm. Concentrations of the 1-2LG nanosheet fractions in water were measured in terms of the lyophilised dry weight of each fraction.

Samples (50 µl of 10 µg/ml) of graphene nanomaterials were placed on 400-mesh carbon-coated copper grids (EM Resolutions, UK). Excess sample was removed after 1 min by wicking with a piece of filter paper. TEM grids were dried overnight at room temperature, in a clean environment. The TEM and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were taken with a Thermo Fisher FEI Talos F200A operated at 200 kV. Raw data were first processed using ImageJ into (.png) files with brightness enhanced by 10 %. Size distributions of individual flakes were analysed, and histograms plotted using Originpro 2021b (Academic) software. The lattice spacing of the GNMs was estimated with Gatan Digital Micrograph software.

Samples (20 µl) were drop cast onto freshly-cleaved mica sheets bound to metal disc stubs (Agar Scientific AGG250-2). Mica sheets were heated up to 100 °C prior to the addition of the samples to avoid any aggregation. Excess sample was washed off the substrate after 2 min with water (2 mL) from a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore, USA), when the remaining water was removed by wicking with a piece of filter paper and by drying overnight at room temperature in a clean environment. Measurements were taken by a Multimode 8 AFM (Bruker, UK) using ScanAsyst Air probes in ScanAsyst mode, with imaging settings at 512 lines/scan, 5 μm scan size, 1 Hz scan rate and 250 mV amplitude set point. All the images were analysed using Nanoscope Analysis 1.9 software (Bruker). Images were flattened (second order) to remove background slopes.

Samples were prepared by drop-casting 100 µl of each dispersion onto a silicon substrate, and by drying overnight under vacuum at 40 °C. Black FLG samples were lyophilised and sent for similar XPS analysis. XPS spectra were recorded with an ESCA2SR spectrometer (ScientaOmicron GmbH, France) using monochromated Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV, 20 mA emission at 300 W, 1 mm spot size) with a base vacuum pressure of ∼ 1 × 10−9 mbar. Charge neutralisation was achieved using a low energy electron flood source (FS40A, PREVAC SP. Z O.O., Poland). Analysis and curve fitting was performed using Casa XPS 2.3.19 software. Binding energy scale calibration was performed using 285 eV, 532 eV, and 399 eV as references in the C 1s, O1s and N1s spectra, respectively, followed by a U 2 Tougaard baseline subtraction and Gaussian–Lorentzian (70:30) fitting for all functional groups.

Graphene nanomaterials were analysed using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 2 instrument (PerkinElmer LAS Ltd., UK) equipped with a diamond universal Attenuated Total Reflectance [30] accessory. Four scans were recorded for each sample at a resolution of 2 cm−1 over a wavenumber region of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1.

Graphene nanomaterial dispersions in D2O were used instead of H2O for NMR measurements, thereby avoiding overwhelming signal interference from H2O. 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker AVANCE II + 400 Ultra Shield NMR spectrometer (9.4 Tesla) operating at proton and carbon frequencies of 400 MHz and 100 MHz, respectively, using a 5 mm BBI 1H/D-BB Z-GRD Z8202/0347 probe. In all cases, spectra were acquired and processed using Bruker TopSpin 2.1® software (Version 2.1). 1H and 13C NMR chemical shifts (δ) are reported in parts per million (ppm).

Samples were prepared by dropping 50 µl of the dispersion onto glass microscope slides, dried overnight under vacuum at 40 °C. Raman spectra were recorded using an InVia microscope (Renishaw plc., UK) fitted with a 100X objective lens, a 532 nm (2.33 eV) laser operating at 1 mW, a grating of 2400 l/mm, and with 15 accumulations of 10 s exposure times. The spectrometer was calibrated using a silicon sample with characteristic peak at 520 cm−1. Interference from fluorescence was removed using Wire 4.4 software. Raman peaks were fitted to a Lorentzian function using Originpro 2021b (Academic) software.

A Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) estimated ζ of aqueous dispersions of graphene nanomaterials. Samples were diluted 10 to 50-fold to fill disposable capillary cells (Malvern DTS1070) avoiding bubbles, followed by equilibration for 120 s before measurements in normal resolution mode. ζ values were calculated using the Smoluchowski function as averages ± SD of three measurements.

UV–VIS spectra were recorded using a Varian Cary 4000 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia) at 25 °C, over the wavelength range 200 to 800 nm in optical grade quartz cuvettes with 1 cm path length. Samples were diluted 10–50-fold in distilled water before measurement. Distilled water was used as blank.

Photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra of aqueous dispersions of GNMs were recorded in 1 cm path length quartz cuvettes using a Shimadzu RF-5301 Spectro fluorophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) operating under RF-5310PC software, with temperature-controlled (25 °C) cell holder and 150 W Xenon lamp.

The quantum yield of photoluminescent nanosheets and dots (QY) was estimated in 5 dilutions with absorbance of less than 0.1 by using quinine sulphate (QS) as a reference (QYR 0.54 at 360 nm in 0.1 M H2SO4):

Grad refers to the slope of the linear curve of absorbance values versus integrated area of fluorescence intensity of QS and GQDs produced at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The refractive index (η) for the sample in water was 1.333 and 1.427 for QS in sulfuric acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of different small 1–2 layer graphene nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Ultrasonic exfoliation in NMP, even at low power, yielded a rich polydispersity of graphene nanomaterials, with not only a broad distribution of nanosheet thicknesses and lateral sizes, but also with distinct chemistries associated with the different sizes, reflecting underlying mechanisms and different extents of exposure to gas radicals. Mechanical mechanisms of FLG exfoliation and methods of its characterisation are well reported, including by microscopy to characterise different sizes within polydisperse mixtures [16,17]. Otherwise, polydisperse mixtures of species of different sizes and properties are typically characterised, when key differences between populations become hidden within the average. From the typical size distribution around 100 nm, dominated by multi- and few-layer graphene nanosheets [16,17], here we isolated multiple fractions of small 1–2 layer graphene (1-2LG) nanosheets in the quantum-confined edge effects region, down to quantum dots of 1–2 nm (Fig. 1 ).

The yellow-coloured supernatant of smaller 1-2LG nanosheets and dots had photoluminescence reminiscent of CQDs [13–15]. Evaporation of NMP left a yellow waxy solid, which was readily soluble in water. Rather than leaving this polydisperse mixture of smaller 1-2LG nanosheets and dots unfractionated, we used dialysis to isolate 1-2LG nanosheets that had self-assembled, followed by high performance size-exclusion chromatography (SEC) of the dialysed and retained fractions in order to separate and isolate 2D nanosheet sizes from 25 nm down to 0D dots around 1–2 nm. Dialysis has been widely used as a single-step purification of carbon-based nanomaterials, notably to retain the residual carbon or graphite, and dialyse the CQDs or GQDs together with solutes [30]. Here, we used a 12 kDa molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) dialysis membrane to collect, from the yellow non-sedimented fraction, the larger assemblies retained (R) by the membrane and the smaller nanosheets and dots permeating through the membrane into the dialysate (D). Both retained (R) assemblies and dialysed (D) nanosheets and dots were lyophilised, when each separated into 3 further different fractions by SEC (Fig. 1 and Supplementary Figure S4 ). Neither dialysis nor size-exclusion chromatography was likely to distinguish any difference in hydrodynamic size between nanosheets of the same lateral diameter but with different thicknesses from multiple layers of graphene. Although thicker nanosheets have higher molecular weight, their lateral diameters and related hydrodynamic sizes would be too similar. Similarly, if the self-assembly of nanosheets was neatly face-to-face there could also be insufficient difference in the hydrodynamic size of the aggregate. However, if self-assembly was edge-to-edge or face-to-face but offset laterally, the lateral diameter of the aggregate and its hydrodynamic size would increase to be retained by dialysis and SEC column chromatography, as observed here.

All fractions contributed to the yellow colour, except for the first fraction (RF1) in the series retained by dialysis (Fig. 1), which were small black FLG nanosheets not sedimented at 58,000g, and possibly involved in more buoyant assemblies. The yellow nanosheet isolates retained despite extensive dialysis (RF2 and RF3) were of smaller individual lateral size than other nanosheets passing into the dialysate. Consequently, their retention by dialysis was likely because of self-assembly into at least an edge-to-edge dimer or likely a multimer with at least twice the lateral size (RF2) from its column retention time, in equilibrium with possible monomer isolate (RF3), diluted from the assemblies during elution from the column (but not by dialysis). The dialysate separated into three distinct size fractions by SEC: substantially dots (DF3), but also distinct small nanosheets (DF2) and larger dialysing nanosheets (DF1), which were likely multimeric assemblies, suggested by their column retention times and peak broadening (Fig. 1 and Supplementary Figure S4 ). Each of these nanosheet and dot fractions were lyophilised and then dissolved in water for further characterisation. All fractions remained well dispersed in water (or NMP), with no discernible aggregation into sediments after 2 years.

Multi-layer and few layer graphene nanosheets were typical of those produced by ultrasonic exfoliation of graphite flakes [16,17]. Here, TEM confirmed our separation of this typical polydisperse mixture and isolation of different-sized fractions. Notably, each isolated fraction had a comparatively narrow lateral size range, with little overlap with neighbouring isolates of different size, which provided stepwise descent into the quantum-confined region from 2D nanosheets down to 0D dots. The 1-2LG nanosheet isolates also had different shapes, either with fragmented sharp-angular or with more rounded edges, indicative of sonochemical ablation of sharp edges (Fig. 2 ). This confirmation of the isolation of 1-2LG with distinct differences in size and shape provided the opportunity here to characterise the chemical differences between these isolates also, which otherwise would be hidden in the average of typical polydisperse mixtures.

The black, larger FLG (LG) nanosheets isolated at 17,000 g generally had a size distribution of 75 ± 9.2 nm (Fig. 2 a). The small black graphene (RF1), not sedimented at 58,000 g and isolated here by SEC had a nanosheet-like shape, but which was less sharp at the edges (Fig. 2 b), with a size distribution of 24.9 ± 5.2 nm (Fig. 2 b). The yellow photoluminescent isolates had a more circular uniform shape (Fig. 2 c-h), presumably resulting from sonochemical erosion. TEM illustrated that lateral size exclusion dominated hydrodynamic separation in the range from 25 nm to 1 nm by high performance SEC, as well as by differential centrifugation. The lateral sizes of fractions in the dialysate (D) were separated according to their sizes.

Interestingly, the dialysate sizes were similar (DF2, 5.1 ± 1.5 nm in Fig. 2 f; DF3, 1.8 ± 0.5 nm in Fig. 2 g) or even larger (DF1, 14.6 ± 2.5 nm in Fig. 2 e), than the lateral sizes of the retained (R) fractions (RF2, 4.8 ± 0.9 nm in Fig. 2 c; RF3, 4.4 ± 1.5 nm in Fig. 2 d). TEM confirmed that the yellow fractions retained by dialysis were comprised of 1-2LG nanosheets of similar lateral size (Fig. 2 h), when the larger hydrodynamic size of RF2 can only be explained by the assembly of multimers remaining upon chromatographic dilution, whose larger lateral dimensions dominated the retention by dialysis membranes. The possible nature of the multimer assemblies is considered later alongside differences in edge modification, zeta potential and spectral shifts from assembly.

HR-TEM resolved the well-reported [13,33–35] characteristic crystalline lattice spacings, which were similar for all the isolated layered graphene nanosheets. Examples are shown of lattice fringes measured to be 0.24 nm both for larger (75 nm) few-layer graphene (LG in Fig. 2a) with 2–3 layers (from AFM, Fig. 3 a) and for very small (1–2 nm) nanosheets (Fig. 2g) with 1–2 layers (from AFM, Fig. 3g), which correspond to the (1120) crystal phase of graphite [13,33,34]. The other spacings (0.21 and 0.22 nm) appear consistent with the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon of graphene [35]. The isolates characterised here had structural core more similar to their crystalline graphitic graphene origin, than GQDs originated from highly-oxidised graphitic graphene and amorphous carbon [13].

The topography and thickness of the nanosheets was investigated by AFM, which were not provided by TEM, as sizes were too small to be suspended over vacuum. Larger FLG (LG, Fig. 3 a) had topographical height distributed in the range 0.9 nm to 1.4 nm, suggesting that LG consisted of 2–3 layers. The heights of small 1-2LG (SG from RF1 Fig. 3 b) and smaller nanosheets and dots (Fig. 3 c to Fig. 3 g) were lower than LG and ranged from 0.5 nm in the smallest dialysate fraction (DF3, Fig. 3 g) to around 1 nm, indicating 1 to 2 graphene layers. The lateral size from TEM scaled in proportion to thickness (number of layers, Fig. 3 h) estimated by AFM. Larger lateral sizes were the result of FLG fragmentation into nanosheets. Smaller lateral sizes may have arisen from fragmentation of their edge regions.

These smaller fragments appear subjected to greater sonochemical erosion in proximity to cavitating bubbles, which may have preferentially affected their sharp-reactive edges, progressively rounding their shapes. The more angular edge shapes of larger nanosheets, compared to the rounded edges of smaller nanosheets, indicates that the smaller nanosheets had been subjected to much more sonochemical erosion and associated modification than larger nanosheets. The smaller nanosheets, including the (also modified) smaller black FLG (RF1) nanosheets appear to be of size preferentially nucleating cavitation bubbles [36]. Their reactive edges could then be more readily accessed in proximity to the short-lived radicals of a bubble nucleated and imploding at the small nanosheet. In contrast, any bubble implosion along the surface of larger few-layer graphene would have resulted in less radical attack, with fewer edges and dangling C bonds in the immediate proximity.

NMR spectroscopy has been little used to characterise graphene nanomaterials. Magic-angle spinning NMR of these solid-phase GNMs has poor resolution compared to other solid-phase characterisations, such as XPS that was also used here. In contrast, we are here interested in the solution phase, where higher resolution proton and carbon NMR spectroscopy revealed particular sonochemical modifications of the isolated nanosheets (Fig. 4 ).

Four regions of 1H NMR were identified: N-substituted aliphatic functional groups (e.g. −N-CH3, −N-CH2- and −N-CH-) at 2–3 ppm, O-substituted functionalities (e.g. next to ether, ester, hydroxyl or epoxide groups) at 2.5 – 4.5 ppm, alkene protons at 4.5–7 ppm and aromatic protons at 6.5–8.4 ppm [37]. 13C NMR spectroscopy (Fig. 4 right) distinguished sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and confirmed the presence of carbon atoms attached to different O- and N-substituted functional groups. The peaks below 100 ppm are ascribed to sp3 hybridised carbon of ether, alcohol, epoxide groups or aliphatic (tertiary and/or secondary) amines, while the peaks from 100 to 180 ppm are attributed to sp2 carbons of alkenes, ketones, esters, aromatic systems, pyridinic modifications, carboxylic acids or amides [38]. Aliphatic N-substitutions were clearly evident here for all fractions isolated, appearing in the 13C resonance region at 20–65 ppm (Fig. 4 right), presumably largely at the edges, as they progressively increased as the size decreased. An appearance of a few downfield 13C signals between 120 ppm and 160 ppm suggested the possibility of occasional pyridinic modifications, which are also more frequent for the smaller fractions RF3, DF2 and DF3. These aliphatic amines and aromatic pyridinic modifications would counter the substantially negative charge of 1-2LG nanosheets in water. 13C signals seen at 50–90 ppm in the aliphatic region of 13C NMR spectra signified O-substitutions into sp3 carbon. Distortionless Enhancement by Polarization Transfer (DEPT) NMR, using 1H→13C resonance transfer pulse sequences to detect the number of protons at each carbon, clearly indicated the presence of primary, secondary and tertiary carbons, and was used here to confirm the presence of different functional groups in the isolated nanosheet and dot fractions (Supplementary Figure S5-S9 ).

Direct radical addition reaction into sp3 carbon was apparent for N and O atoms, which increased as the size of the dialysed fractions became smaller (DF1 < DF2 < DF3 for sp3 C-N and C-O, Fig. 4, bottom right), as the contribution of edge modification was greater in smaller nanosheets. Carbonyl carbons of ketone, ester, carboxylic acid or amide groups emerged in the sp2 hybridized carbon region of the 13C NMR spectra at around 180 ppm (Fig. 4 right) mainly in the smaller dialyzed isolates (i.e. DF2 and especially DF3). In contrast, a greater contribution from O-substituted aliphatic functional groups was witnessed for the larger-sized dialysed fraction (DF1) in both the 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 4), thus suggesting that their further fracture along these O-substituted nanosheet weaknesses and further conversion into carbonyl-containing groups (to generate DF2 and DF3) may have included cumulative H. and OH· radical attack from water vapour, alongside continuing O⋅ and N. radical additions at their fresh edges.

Differences were apparent, however, for the nanosheets retained by dialysis in likely multimer assembly (RF2) and in the monomer released during chromatography (RF3), which were generally less modified with similar functionalities. Both had less N and O atom addition into sp3 carbon than the dialysing fractions, particularly than the similarly-sized DF2, and less O atom addition even than the larger-sized DF1 (Fig. 4, top right). The multimerising RF2 showed almost complete absence of carbonyl groups which, though apparent in the monomer RF3 eluting from co-assembly with RF2, were less prominent than in the dialysed fractions, even than in the larger-sized dialysing isolate DF1. The multimerising RF2 had the least modification, with negligible conversion of O-substitutions into carbonyl and carboxyl groups to replace negative charge, lost to weakly basic N-additions of newly generated amines and pyridinic modifications, thus likely leading to H+ rather than the typical OH– interactions providing the net negative charge of unmodified nanosheet surfaces. Presumably, these differences promoted self-assembly: positively-charged and less-negatively charged edge regions would be more likely to overcome the otherwise negative charge-driven repulsion between graphene nanosheet faces, which maintained the colloidal stability of the nanosheets in water. The multimerising RF2 nanosheet isolate would then be better suited for self-assembly, whereas monomer RF3 diluted out of the multimer did have low carboxylation of its edges, while the dialysing fractions (DF1, DF2, DF3) had progressively more carboxyl modification, alongside the greater N and O additions. These less hydrophilic multimerising nanosheet isolates (RF2 and RF3), with amphiphilic aromatic and aliphatic weakly basic N-additions at their sheet edges may have been better suited to nucleating gas bubbles from dissolved nitrogen and oxygen from air. This suggests that they may be precursor fragments of the smaller dialysed nanosheet isolates (DF2 and DF3), which were subjected to continued sonochemical modification.

XPS spectroscopy analysed differences in the chemical compositions [13,22,39] resulting from different sonochemical modifications of graphene. Additional to graphitic carbon (C 1s peak at 285 eV), XPS predominantly showed (Fig. 5
a) oxygen (O 1s peak at 532 eV) and nitrogen (N 1s peak at 400 eV).

Remarkably, the N peak was hardly noticeable in the spectrum of larger FLG (LG) nanosheets, which implies that graphene was only weakly modified with nitrogen. Black nanosheets had high proportions of carbon (90.3 % for LG) with reduced carbon contribution in the smaller black 1-2LG nanosheets (87.8 % for RF1). Otherwise, the larger and thicker fraction not retained by dialysis (DF1) also had a higher carbon content (81.7 %), suggestive of formation from fragments with an island of few-layer graphene, where the inner layer(s) would be more protected from sonochemical attack and subsequent modification.

In contrast, smaller and thinner nanosheets had much lower carbon contents (62.8 % for DF3), consistent with greater sonochemical erosion and functionalisation at their eroded edges, which agrees with the above 1H and 13C NMR data. High resolution XPS C 1s spectra (Supplementary Figure S10 ) deconvoluted three peaks assigned to C = C at 284.8 eV, with C-(O, N) at 286.2 eV elevated for small 1-2LG (RF1) and the dot (DF3) fractions, and O-C = O/O = C-NH2 at 287.8 eV elevating in smaller nanosheet and dot fractions, which is consistent with their 13C NMR spectra indicating increased level of carbonyl carbons [13].

FTIR spectroscopy further confirmed the different sonochemical edge modifications of the FLG fragments (Fig. 5 b). A strong vibrational absorption band at 1653 cm−1 was observed for all fractions, which was attributed to C = C stretch of aromatic rings and/or carbonyl groups. Characteristic aromatic vibration stretch of C–H bonds at 2937 cm−1 and 2869 cm−1 were also features of all fractions, which was present also in smaller 1-2LG (RF1), but not in larger FLG (LG) nanosheets.

Corresponding to the stretching vibration of NH/OH groups, the broader peak at 3289 cm−1 was a distinctive feature of the largest (DF1) and smallest (DF3) nanosheet isolates in the dialysate, and also of the multimerising RF2 fraction, compared to other fractions. XPS N 1s spectra indicated the greater NH amide levels in the same fractions (Supplementary Figure S11 ). Similarly, peaks at 1407 cm−1 ascribed to C–N were strongly apparent for the larger (DF1) and smaller (DF3) dialysate fractions. Reciprocally, all except LG and the larger retained (RF2) and dialysed (DF1) fractions showed a peak at 1770 cm−1 attributed to carbonyl groups, which was also consistent with 13C NMR, where RF2 and DF1 had less contribution to carbonyl groups compared to other fractions. Stretching vibration of the C-O group at 1036 cm−1 from O. addition appeared in all isolates.

The N or O atom addition reactions of the smaller nanosheets with size preferentially nucleating cavitation bubbles [36] suggest their close proximity to cavitation, before atomic N⋅ and O⋅ radicals were lost to further reactions and sonochemical products. Despite the greater extent of further chemical modification of the small nanosheets and dots at their edges, extensive graphene-ordered carbon remained, presumably with graphene properties at their centre, which are degraded in the extensively-oxidised GO [42] and amorphous carbon [43].

Raman spectroscopy reported the extent of crystallinity retained in the different nanosheets isolated. The first order Raman spectra D and G bands emerged at about 1351 and 1579 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 5 c). The G band is due to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon in graphitic carbon layer [40], whilst the disordered/defect-related D band can be attributed to reduction in size, resulting increased edge effect and the associated functional groups [39]. The intensity ratio of D over G bands (Fig. 5 c ID/IG inset, Supplementary Table S1 ) thereby scales relative disorder.

Larger FLG (LG) nanosheets retained order with relatively low ID/IG of 0.4, rising with smaller 1-2LG (RF1) to 0.62, similar to the larger dialysate (DF1) isolate at 0.6 and retained (RF2) isolate at 0.72, representing the less-modified nanosheet isolates. However, the smaller and more modified nanosheet isolates had higher ID/IG > 1, with considerable broadening in the G (full width at half maximum, FWHMG) and D (FWHMD) bands. The increased ID/IG could also be due to the smaller size confirmed by TEM images, as smaller nanosheet fractions have higher ID/IG [44] and edge effect [45,46]. As the nanosheets fragmented into smaller sizes, the breaking of the transitional symmetry of the lattice was increasingly due to the edge defects activating the D band [14]. The modifications with highly electronegative nitrogen- and oxygen-based functional groups, as confirmed by NMR, XPS and FTIR result in increased electronic charge, which likely caused the broadening of the D and G bands [14,47]. This can also cause the redshift of the G band [47]; indeed, significant redshift was apparent (Fig. 5 c) of the G band of smaller nanosheet fractions (RF3 of 22 cm−1 and DF3 of 30 cm−1).

Taken together with wider characterisation, our Raman findings here indicate that the changes in the D band were due to their smaller size and increased edge effect rather than basal plane defects, as the ID/IG was less than 2 [48]. The intensity of the second order 2D band at 2700 cm−1 became weaker with increasing ID/IG (Fig. 5 c), because of the modifications with increased electron concentration, consequently causing extra scattering [49].

Zeta potential (ζ) reported the net electrical charge resultant of any ionisation of functional groups [18] and, particularly here, charge transfer between the material and solvent [50]. The larger FLG (LG) nanosheets (75 nm) had the highest ζ of −59.7 mV at pH 7 (Fig. 5 d). Charge transfer from OH– of water molecules adsorbed to nanosheet faces [38] is the only likely explanation because LG was minimally-modified, confirmed by XPS, FTIR and NMR (e.g., through the lack 1H NMR signals) with relatively few negatively-charged oxygen-based functional groups, and the absence of amine groups. The reduction in negative zeta potential of small black 1-2LG (25 nm) RF1 (ζ of −40.8 mV) and all smaller nanosheets with greater positive shift in ζ was most likely due to the presence of aliphatic amine and pyridinic groups that can be fully or partly protonated. Loss of OH– interaction at the modified edges would accompany gain of positive charge at the edges, which would counter the central negative charge of the unmodified nanosheet.

The greatest drop in negative zeta potential was seen for the larger dialysing DF1 isolate (ζ of −28.8 mV) reaching the point of colloidal instability, but which showed a significant degree of aromatic and aliphatic N atom modification, evident from 1H and 13C NMR (Fig. 4) and FTIR (Fig. 5 b). These groups would be protonated with positive edge charge in water, which countered the negative charge of unmodified nanosheet surfaces, and appears to have been the largest contributor to the decrease in negative zeta potential. Whereafter the further oxidative conversion, evident from the appearance of carbonyl groups, both (1) decreased positive charge by replacing amines with neutral amides (Fig. 4) and (2) increased negative charge by forming carboxyl groups. Together, this increased ionic negative charge, evident for the dialysing DF2 and especially the DF3 isolates, but which only led to a partial “recovery” of the negative zeta potential, illustrating that the graphene faces rather than carboxyl groups dominated the negative zeta potential. The smallest dialysate fraction (DF3) showed the greater recovery with ζ of −36.7 (Fig. 5 d), reflecting the greater proportion of ionisable carboxyl groups at its edges, as well as greater loss of amines to amides. However, even here, the graphene faces dominated the overall negative zeta potential.

Despite their smaller size and > 3 fold greater edge, the smaller nanosheet isolates retained by dialysis (RF2 and RF3) had less O and N addition than the least modified of the dialysing isolates DF1 (from NMR, Fig. 4). Although the multimerising RF2 had O-substitutions into sp3 carbon (e.g. neutral ether), little conversion into the carbonyl containing groups was evident in the aliphatic regions of 1H and 13C NMR spectra, even for the monomer RF3 diluted from the multimer during SEC (Fig. 4). Less of the positive charge from their tertiary or secondary amines would have been lost to neutral amides (e.g., through oxidative amidation with molecular oxygen) in the isolates retained by dialysis, than in the larger dialysing isolate, DF1 and also DF2, which also had more O and N additions. Despite their lesser OH– charge transfer from water and their multimeric assembly, compared to the larger (LG) and smaller 1-2LG (RF1), colloidal stability remained for more than two years without discernible aggregation into sediment.

The effects of the widely-varying characteristics on possible mechanism underlying multicolour luminescence are not well understood for graphene. Notable mechanistic consideration of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), derived from graphene oxide with substantial basal plane as well as edge modifications, highlighted the multiple electronic transitions of diverse chemical modifications and the formation of edge-to-edge (J-type) aggregates with red-shifted excitations and emissions [52]. Obviously, heterogeneous dispersions of different sizes, chemical modifications and aggregation states make it difficult to deconvolute any particular effects on possible mechanisms from a mixture of different effects. Here, we exfoliated and isolated pristine 1-2LG nanosheets of different sizes with narrow size distributions at the transition into photoluminescence, each with different types and extents of edge modification and self-assembly.

The small black nanosheet (24.9 nm) isolate RF1 was just above the transition into smaller yellow nanosheets starting from the 14.6 nm lateral size of the DF1 isolate, with substantial sp2 graphene structure at its centre and only a relatively small (6 %) increase in O and N atom addition (from 12 % in RF1 to 18 % in DF1 by XPS, Fig. 5 a), despite its larger increase (∼35 %) in edge. However, the least-modified yellow nanosheet isolates RF2 and RF3 were retained by dialysis and by SEC in multimers of similar monomer size (RF2 of 4.8 nm and RF3 of 4.4 nm). Even the larger dialysed nanosheet isolate DF1 with a little more chemical modification, showed a broader peak ( Figure S4 ), suggestive of self-assembly, which was not apparent for the more chemically modified, smallest dot-like nanosheet isolate (DF3 1–2 nm).

Both large (LG) and small (RF1) nanosheet isolates had the broad black absorption expected of graphene through to the weaker absorption beyond 500 nm into red wavelengths. The multimerising RF2 isolate had the least modification (Fig. 5 a) of the smaller yellow nanosheets and was the only isolate with absorption beyond the UV into the violet and blue, extending weakly into green, suggestive of broader graphene absorption. This significant visible absorption was lost from the similarly-sized RF3, which was more N (amine) modified, but with comparatively higher levels of amide and carboxyl groups. Similarly, with greater extents of modifications in the dialysing isolates, including amide and carboxyl groups, there was no extended absorption into the visible region (Fig. 6
a).

The typical single characteristic UV absorption peak at 270 nm for LG (Fig. 6 a, right inset), corresponding to the π- π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond was blue shifted in smaller 1-2LG (RF1) and yellow nanosheets (Fig. 6 a) by approximately 40 nm. The covalent modification of these fractions with O and N atoms likely exerted strong electron affinity, enhancing the electron delocalisation.

The absorption band corresponding to the n- π* transition also shifted as the modifications increased: from broadly around 300 nm for the larger nanosheets (310 nm in DF1 of 14.9 nm size to 300 nm in the less modified RF2 of 4.9 nm size), narrowing down and peaking at about 285 nm for the smaller and more modified nanosheets (RF3 of 4.4 nm, DF2 of 5.1/6.1 nm, and DF3 of 1–2 nm). This blue shift to lower wavelength could be related indirectly to smaller sizes, but certainly increased with the greater extents of N and O modifications (from 1H and 13C NMR, XPS and FTIR in Figs. 4 and 5) in these isolates, which were also generally smaller in size. This shift likely originated from the effect of non-bonding electrons in O and N atoms [26,47,48]. Notably, for the smallest dot-like nanosheets (DF3) with the highest content of O and N, with amide and carboxy groups, the absorption at 285 nm was stronger than others.

Neither the larger (LG) nor the smaller (SG in RF1, 24.95 nm size) of the black nanosheets showed any PL at any wavelength, despite the further N and O modification of SG, when the band gap may still have been too small (Supplementary Table S2 ). Further down into the quantum-confined edge effects region, only those smaller isolates (< 15 nm size), which were yellow in colour in ambient light, emitted blue luminescence when irradiated by a 360 nm lamp (Fig. 6 a, left inset). The multimerising RF2 eluted immediately after the non-luminescent RF1 (SG), with only slightly smaller hydrodynamic assembly size and small differences in the extent of O and N modification. Even though the RF2 monomer size (4.8 nm) was about three times smaller than the little more modified DF1 (14.6 nm) isolate (see Fig. 4), RF2 emission was less than DF1 (Fig. 6 g), suggesting that the modifications had the stronger effect within the photoluminescent quantum confinement regime.

The UV excitation-independent PL emission was comparatively small, whereas the excitation-dependent PL emission characteristic, generally reported for very different materials (CQDs and GO GQDs [53]) dominated. Its emission maxima underwent red shifts to lower energy with progressive decline in intensity as the excitation wavelength was increased to lower energy (Fig. 6 b to 6f), suggestive of mechanistic relationship to the energies of photons incident on the nanosheets. Despite isolates here having different types of modifications, no new excitation peaks appeared. Excitation here lacked the additional excitation maxima reported for graphene oxide GQDs in the violet through into blue and cyan, which were linked to multiple chemical modifications with different types of electronic transitions between anti-bonding and bonding molecular orbitals in N-doped GQDs and different excitation-dependent emission [53].

The overall PL emission behaviour here was more similar to N-doped CQDs with abundant oxygen-rich functional groups [26–28,47,49–51] than N-doped GQD dispersions [53] from graphene oxide (GO) of much larger size range (up to 40–80 nm) than considered here. Nanosheets retained (R) by dialysis had an emission maximum under excitation at 360 nm, which dominated for the smaller RF3 isolate, whereas the dialysed (D) nanosheets had an emission maximum under excitation at 340 nm, whose intensity increased with sonochemical modification, with increasing carboxyl and amide groups (DF3 > DF2 > DF1, Fig. 6 h), while the nanosheet sizes were progressively diminishing. The least modified larger RF2 isolate retained by dialysis (Fig. 5 a) had O and N modifications into aliphatic sp3-hybridised carbons (Fig. 4), but the least sp2 hybridised carbonyl carbon of carboxyl and amide groups, and also had the weaker PL emission. Interestingly, this RF2 fraction showed two excitation maxima at 340 nm and 360 nm, indicating the presence of aggregates with possible transitions between them (Fig. 6 h). This multimerising isolate presented with the remnants of graphene-like visible absorption, suggesting substantial graphene structure, as also indicated by Raman (Fig. 5 b). Its protonated amine and pyridine-like edge modifications provided attractive positive ionic charge to overcome the negative charge screening of the zeta potential of the nanosheet faces and thereby allowed the closer approach needed for face-to-face assembly. The small blue shift in excitation suggests a net H-type face-to-face assembly for this RF2 isolate, whereupon energy loss upon exciton transfer would result in a further red-shifted PL emission for the assemblies.

The PL excitation spectra (PLE) of all nanosheets emitting at 440 nm (Fig. 6 h) showed two peaks, indicating two types of electronic transition, which can be regarded as from σ and π orbitals in the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO). This kind of transition could originate from a triplet carbene-like state, which is more common at the zigzag edges [51]. The carbene triplet ground state contains one electron at σ1 and another electron at π1 ground state, where the energy difference (δE) calculated from PLE between σ and π should be less than 1.5 eV [52,53]. Here, the δE between σ and π was calculated to be 1.1 eV, consistent with reported PL emission mechanism originating from triple carbene at zigzag edges [12,13,33,54,55].

The quantum yield (QY) of nanosheets transitioning into PL emission was estimated by using acidic quinine sulfate (QY = 58 %) as a reference and upon excitation at three different wavelengths (340, 350 and 360 nm) with only a slight difference between wavelengths (Supplementary Figure S14 and Table S3 ). QY under 360 nm excitation was less than 3 % for the larger and less modified nanosheets at the early stage of transition into PL, which were also in assemblies with a lossy red shift (QY = 2.5 % for RF2; 2.8 % for DF1), but progressively increased (QY = 3.9 % for RF3; 3.5 % for DF2) to about 5 % as the nanosheet size decreased (QY = 5.2 % for DF3). The higher QY of the smallest nanosheet isolate DF3 can be attributed to the high content of strong electron-withdrawing N and O atoms [37,55–57]. It is noteworthy that the QY of these dot-like nanosheets was higher, when purified and isolated here than previously reported GQDs dispersions produced by ultrasonication [13].

The photoluminescence (PL) emission peaks of the 1–2 layer graphene (1-2LG) nanosheets red-shifted towards longer wavelengths as the concentrations of their aqueous solutions were increased. Interestingly, the 1-2LG nanosheet isolates had different red shift behaviours in their concentration-dependent photoluminescence (Fig. 7 ). The nanosheet isolate sizes and differing types and extents of chemical modification here changed the range of this red-shifting multicolour photoluminescence (PL) emission.

Nanosheet size and chemical modifications can lead to complex contributions to the observed red shift in their photoluminescent emissions as each nanosheet concentration was increased, including energy-transfer within self-assembling complexes and optical inner filter effects (IFE). The higher optical absorption and greater Rayleigh light scattering at shorter wavelengths can act as a high pass filter here and shift wideband excitation and the resulting excitation-dependent photoluminescence to longer and less scattered wavelengths by IFE. However, we observed comparatively small differences in optical absorption between the different photoluminescent nanosheet isolates (Fig. 6a), when this IFE would be expected to be too similar here between the different isolates at the same concentration to explain the differences in red shifts. The greater Rayleigh scattering of shorter wavelengths would also be similar here for the different isolates: although larger nanosheets would scatter more incident light than smaller nanosheets, the differences in size were not large, and the larger-sized nanosheets were at lower number concentration because they were at the same mass (dry weight) concentration. However, given the possibility of small differences, excitation here was monochromated (bandwidth ≤ 5 nm), when optical wavelength filtering would be insignificant, and IFE could only decrease the intensity of excitation of photoluminescence to similar extents between different nanosheets. Although differences in the size and chemical modifications between nanosheet isolates resulted in comparatively small changes in the photoluminescence emission peaks (Fig. 6g), differences in the extent and/or structural nature of concentration-dependent self-assembly of nanosheets can be related below to the differences in edge chemistry and lateral size of the nanosheets.

Unlike the behaviour of comparatively heterogenous dispersions of GQDs from GO or small CQDs, larger red shift and range of emission colours were enabled here by the more homogenous isolates, particularly those with the greater extent of sp2 graphene and the lesser extent of covalent edge modification. Oxidation into negatively-charged carboxyl groups at the edges, which prevail in GQDs and CQDs, impeded both the strength and extent of red-shifted assembly here. As the concentration and assembly increased, the emission maxima of all nanosheet isolates progressively shifted to longer wavelengths (Fig. 7, with Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates), to a much greater extent than for both CQDs [25,39] and GQDs from GO [53]. Importantly, each 1-2LG nanosheet isolate separated here had a different strength and extent of red shift, which would be hidden within the average of heterogenous dispersions. Moreover, mixed assemblies are likely formed in heterogenous dispersions, where each assembly involves a range of sizes and covalent modifications within the dispersion, where face-to-face assembly may be particularly impeded by more modified nanosheets as well as by smaller nanosheets in heterogenous mixtures.

Indeed, the largest (size 14.6 nm) and least-modified, dialysed nanosheet isolate DF1, with the greatest sp2 carbon, exhibited the greatest red shift of 156 nm in its PL emission maxima (Fig. 7 c). Emission colours extended beyond the blue emission in dilute solution into green, yellow and orange with a tail though into red emission as its concentration was increased to modest (1 mg/ml) levels (Fig. 7 h). This red shift was about 50 % larger than for GQDs from GO [53], at five times lower concentration and with a simpler excitation spectrum in the UV and violet, lacking the excitation spectrum spreading into longer wavelengths. The smaller dialysing fractions with greater O and N modification and more amide and carboxyl groups had much smaller red shifts (132 nm for DF2, Fig. 7 d and 75 nm for DF3, in Fig. 7 e), closer to GQD red shifts.

The least O- and N-modified nanosheets (predominantly ether, epoxides and amine groups), retained by dialysis had substantial red shifts (108 nm for RF2 in Fig. 7 a and 93 nm for RF3 in Fig. 7 b), despite their limited range of functional groups and lacking the carboxyls viewed to be important in GQDs [53, 63, 64] and CQDs [26–28,47,49–51]. Clearly, the substantial red shifts of these least-modified nanosheet isolates depended on their strength of self-assembly, and not on the more diverse range of functional groups implicated for GO GQDs [53], which were minimal in these particular isolates (Figs. 4 and 5). Moreover, these isolates had emission tails into the red, unlike the small dot-like isolate, DF3, with the most functional groups, which was here weakest in assembling red shift of its emission (Fig. 7 e). Presumably, the negative ionic charge of carboxyl groups predominated, as amines were replaced by neutral amides, which would lead to greater repulsion between nanosheets at their edges as well as from nanosheet faces, when much higher concentrations were necessary for assembling red shifts, as less graphene nanosheet remained for assembly. Moreover, even this dot-like nanosheet isolate with more functional groups did not have a complex excitation spectrum into longer wavelengths like GQDs from GO [53]; instead, the additional functional groups only blue shifted the excitation spectrum, which was otherwise similar to the least-modified nanosheets (Fig. 6 h) and closer to the behaviour of CQDs [39].

Such restriction of face-to-face, π-π molecular interactions, with decline in zwitterionic edges, as positively-charged amines were replaced with neutral amides, allowed negatively-charged carboxyl groups at the edges of the nanosheets to dominate and repel other nanosheet faces and edges. With negatively-charged edges as well as sp2 graphene faces, face-to-face assembly was likely impeded, when less orderly head-to-tail arrangement of nanosheets, likened to J-type aggregation of organic fluorophores [37,58], here led to weaker red shift in emission. New energy levels with lower energy would appear due to the interaction, narrowing the energy gap and ultimately resulting in such a red shift and decrease in PL emission intensity. There was a near linear relationship between both the size and zeta potential of these nanosheet isolates and the red shifts of their emission maxima as their concentrations were increased. The important outlier was the largest nanosheet DF1 (Supplementary Figure S15 ) with the largest conjugated sp2 domain indicated by TEM (Fig. 2 e) and the smallest zeta potential (Fig. 5 d). Its weakened electrostatic repulsion reduced inter-nanosheet distance and increased assembly strength and the red shift in emission, whose excitation spectrum also indicated net H-type face-to-face assembly at low concentrations. This larger nanosheet isolate likely had a greater strength and extent of assembly because of its larger graphene π system at higher concentrations, ultimately resulting in additional red shift of the emission [37,59,60]. With greater face to face assembly of 1-2LG nanosheets, both H (face to face) and J (edge to edge) type assembly of the photoluminescent modifications at nanosheet edges would arise.

Beyond dilute solutions, self-assembly of nanosheets became the governing parameter in their PL spectra. The strong red shifts were clearly from nanosheets with greater sp2 carbon and less modification, where net face-to-face H-type assembly was indicated for initial assembly. The more modified and smaller nanosheets with less sp2 carbon showed weaker red shift similar to the head-to-tail, J-type assembly of more modified CQDs and GQDs from GO [53]. However, computational modelling of assembly of GQDs from GO, at lower levels of oxidation than observed experimentally, suggest possible face-to-face assembly [63], observed in CQDs when the oxidised groups were strongly chemically reduced and at acidic pH when the negative charge of carboxyl groups was neutralized by protonation [63, 64]. However, most studies do not consider the negative zeta potential of graphene faces interacting with water (Fig. 5 d), which is clearly the source of the colloidal stability of the nanosheets without the ionic charge from carboxyl groups prevalent in graphene oxide. The stronger red shift here was likely the result of the initial O- and N-addition at the nanosheet edges, resulting in positive charge from amine and pyridinic edge groups, which attracted edges to the negative nanosheet faces, as the initial oxidation here was by O-addition into uncharged groups, such as ether and epoxy. Edge positive charge lowered by half to around the critical [63] −30 mV, the otherwise dominating negative zeta potential of graphene sp2 faces in water, and likely promoted initial face to face assembly with net H-type aggregate luminescence. More extensive O- modification with carbonyls of carboxyl groups and amides, neutralising amine groups, was clearly detrimental to assembly. As N modification promoted large red shift assembly, further improvements in multicolour PL emission may be gained by exfoliation of pristine nanosheets in different gaseous environments, including a nitrogen environment to maximise positive charge and minimise oxygen addition and carboxyl functionalisation, which dominate the GQDs from GO and typical CQDs.

1–2 layer graphene (1-2LG) with distinct nanosheet sizes and edge chemistries have been isolated and characterised across the transition of black 1-2LG into photoluminescent 1-2LG. The blue to red emission of red-shifted assembly was much stronger at this transition than for graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots.
a)
Differential centrifugation was only effective at separating isolates of few layer graphene flakes of 75 nm from small 1-2LG nanosheets of 25 nm size. High performance chromatography isolated different assembly sizes of least-modified nanosheets retained by dialysis and different dialysed nanosheets from 15 nm down to 1–2 nm with narrow size distributions and distinctive edge chemistries.
b)
Photoluminescence emerged from initial addition of electronegative nitrogen and oxygen atoms at 1-2LG nanosheet edges with blue-shifted absorption bands, which otherwise retained sp2 graphene with positively-charged primary, secondary and tertiary amine, as well as pyridinic N groups at the nanosheet edges.
c)
Despite O and N atom edge addition, 1-2LG nanosheets of 25 nm size were not photoluminescent, whereas transitional 15 nm nanosheets and nanosheets with visible-band absorption of graphene had the same blue photoluminescence and excitation spectra of more modified smaller nanosheets lacking visible band absorption.
d)
Positively-charged edges of transitional nanosheets countered the repulsion between nanosheets from the negative zeta potential of their sp2 graphene faces in water and promoted initial face-to-face assembly, where nanosheets from 25 nm down to 15 nm with greater N addition and edge positive charge, reduced the zeta potential by up to half and had large red shifts (>150 nm) in photoluminescence.
e)
Carbonyl groups emerged with more extensive sonochemistry, with negatively-charged carboxyl groups and conversion of amines into neutral amides, thus eliminating positive charge at the nanosheet edges. Negative charge at the edges as well as nanosheet faces produced weaker red shifts (75 nm), similar to graphene oxide and carbon quantum dots, with less sp2 carbon and more edge-to-edge assembly.
Graphene photoluminescence can be refined at the transition into quantum confinement of N atom edge effects which avoid oxygen functional groups and retain sp2 graphene faces.
